protocol_refs.title: NREMT Patient Assessment/Management — Trauma Skill Sheet (2024)
NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Trauma (General Trauma Management, Extremity Trauma, Head Injury, Spinal Care)

ems_performed_exam._note: ai_roleplay_tag intentionally excluded: roleplay responses include caregiver/patient-stated findings that are not EMS-performed exams.

challenge_performed_exam._note: Structured challenge-gated exam evidence. Lung sound findings in training must come from the lung sound challenge, not AI prose or transcript fallback.

challenge_calculated_gcs._note: Structured GCS modal result in training. Required for GCS-specific neuro assessment items.

history_obtained._note: Aggregate role — use caregiver_reported or patient_reported when the source distinction matters clinically.

ems_administered_intervention._note: Intervention Tier 1 evidence comes from the intervention table directly. This role is QA/QI metadata for cross-reference.

checklist_items.description: Takes or verbalizes appropriate BSI/PPE precautions before patient contact
checklist_items.done_feedback: BSI/PPE confirmed before patient contact — correct practice for all trauma calls.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: BSI/PPE precautions must be verbalized before approaching any trauma patient. Blood and body fluid exposure is high in trauma — gloves and eye protection are non-negotiable.
common_errors: Approaching patient before verbalizing PPE

checklist_items.description: Determines scene safety before approaching patient
checklist_items.done_feedback: Scene safety assessed — correct before patient contact.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Scene safety must be determined before approaching any trauma patient. An unsecured scene puts both you and the patient at risk.
common_errors: Rushing to patient without completing scene size-up

checklist_items.description: Determines mechanism of injury (MOI) on scene approach; uses MOI to predict injury patterns
checklist_items.done_feedback: MOI determined — guides index of suspicion for injury patterns and informs spinal precaution decision.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: MOI drives the entire trauma assessment: blunt vs. penetrating, high- vs. low-energy, height of fall. Without it you cannot predict injury patterns, apply appropriate spinal precautions, or make an informed transport decision.
common_errors: Documenting 'patient fell' without characterizing mechanism (step vs. ladder, standing vs. moving)

checklist_items.description: Determines total number of patients and requests additional resources if needed
checklist_items.done_feedback: Patient count determined and additional resources requested if indicated.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Always determine the total number of patients on scene approach. Multi-patient incidents require immediate resource requests — early is better than late.
common_errors: Assuming single patient without scanning the scene

checklist_items.description: Considers and verbalizes need for spinal motion restriction based on MOI
checklist_items.done_feedback: Spinal precaution considered — verbalized appropriately based on MOI.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Spinal motion restriction consideration must be verbalized for all significant trauma MOI. Document your reasoning whether or not you apply SMR.
common_errors: Applying SMR without documenting clinical indication
Not verbalizing SMR consideration when MOI warrants it

checklist_items.description: Verbalizes general impression of patient including apparent life threats; determines LOC/responsiveness (AVPU or GCS)
checklist_items.done_feedback: General impression formed and LOC assessed — establishes baseline for trajectory monitoring throughout the call.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: General impression orients your entire response: is the patient talking? Bleeding? Moving? LOC in trauma signals brain injury or shock — establish AVPU/GCS immediately on approach.
common_errors: Skipping general impression in favor of jumping straight to ABCs

checklist_items.description: Opens and assesses airway patency using trauma technique (jaw thrust); inserts airway adjunct as indicated; suctions as needed
checklist_items.done_feedback: Airway assessed and managed appropriately — correct prioritization in trauma primary survey.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Airway is the first clinical priority in the trauma primary survey. Assess patency (open? protected?), suction blood or secretions, insert adjunct if patient cannot protect their airway. Use jaw thrust — not chin-lift — when spinal injury is possible.
common_errors: Using chin-lift instead of jaw thrust when spinal injury is possible
Not suctioning blood or secretions before inserting adjunct

checklist_items.description: Assesses breathing rate and quality; identifies inadequate ventilation requiring intervention
checklist_items.done_feedback: Breathing assessed — rate and quality documented for primary survey.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Breathing assessment in trauma goes beyond rate: assess depth (tidal volume), symmetry, and paradoxical movement. Inadequate breathing — slow rate, shallow depth, or asymmetric rise — requires assisted ventilation. Do not delay.
common_errors: Assessing rate without depth or symmetry
Missing asymmetric chest rise indicating tension pneumothorax or hemothorax

checklist_items.description: Initiates appropriate oxygen therapy; manages life-threatening chest injuries (open chest wound, flail segment)
checklist_items.done_feedback: Oxygen initiated and chest life threats addressed — critical early interventions in trauma.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Oxygen therapy and chest life threat management belong in the primary survey. Seal open chest wounds with a vented dressing; assist ventilation for inadequate breathing; recognize tension PTX signs (absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, JVD) and decompress.
common_errors: Delaying oxygen while completing full assessment
Using non-vented occlusive dressing on open chest wound (risk of tension conversion)

checklist_items.description: Assesses pulse for rate and quality (radial preferred; carotid if radial absent)
checklist_items.done_feedback: Pulse assessed — rate and quality documented for circulation evaluation.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Pulse rate and quality are the primary circulation indicators in trauma. Tachycardia + hypotension = hemorrhagic shock until proven otherwise. A weak or thready radial pulse indicates 30%+ blood volume loss.
common_errors: Checking rate only without documenting quality (strong/weak/thready)

checklist_items.description: Assesses and controls major external hemorrhage using direct pressure, tourniquet, or wound packing as indicated
checklist_items.done_feedback: Major hemorrhage assessed and controlled — correct prioritization of life-threatening bleeding in primary survey.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Major external hemorrhage is a primary survey life threat — not a secondary assessment finding. Apply direct pressure immediately; use tourniquet 2–3 inches proximal to arterial extremity bleeding; use wound packing with direct pressure for junctional wounds.
common_errors: Lifting pressure dressing to check the wound (releases clot)
Delaying tourniquet for arterial extremity bleed

checklist_items.description: Assesses skin color, temperature, and condition (CTC) for early signs of compensated shock
checklist_items.done_feedback: CTC assessed — skin signs provide early warning of compensated shock before vital signs decompensate.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: CTC (color, temperature, condition) detects compensated shock: cool, pale, diaphoretic skin occurs before blood pressure drops. Identify it here — before it becomes decompensated and blood pressure is already lost.
common_errors: Checking capillary refill only without color or temperature

checklist_items.description: Identifies priority patients and makes transport decision (load-and-go vs. treat on scene); targets scene time ≤ 10 minutes for critical trauma
checklist_items.done_feedback: Transport decision made — priority correctly determined after primary survey.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Transport decision must be made after primary survey — not after secondary assessment. For high-MOI or critical trauma: load and go. Scene time > 10 minutes in critical trauma correlates with increased mortality. The OR is definitive care; you are not.
common_errors: Delaying transport to complete secondary assessment on an unstable patient
Not stating trauma center as destination for high-MOI mechanism

checklist_items.description: Obtains chief complaint and pain history (OPQRST) from patient or bystanders
checklist_items.done_feedback: Chief complaint and pain history obtained — characterizes injury complaint for trending and handoff.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Even in trauma, ask about chief complaint and characterize pain using OPQRST. Pain location, onset, and severity guide secondary assessment priorities and reveal injury sites not obvious on inspection.
common_errors: Skipping pain assessment in altered or unconscious patient without documenting inability to obtain

checklist_items.description: Obtains SAMPLE history: signs/symptoms, allergies, medications, pertinent PMH, last oral intake, events preceding
checklist_items.done_feedback: SAMPLE history obtained — allergies and anticoagulant use are particularly critical for trauma management and surgical planning.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: SAMPLE history is surgical planning data: anticoagulants dramatically increase hemorrhage risk and affect OR decisions; allergies affect anesthesia choices; last meal timing matters for airway management and surgery timing.
common_errors: Skipping allergies when patient is unstable — still required for documentation and hospital prep

checklist_items.description: Inspects and palpates head, scalp, and facial structures; assesses pupils bilaterally for size, equality, and reactivity
checklist_items.done_feedback: Head, face, and pupils assessed — TBI signs (pupil inequality, mechanism, LOC change) and facial trauma identified.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Full head assessment: scalp (lacerations, deformities, hematoma), skull (step-off, crepitus), face (symmetry, PERRL, facial bone stability), ears (Battle's sign), nose (CSF rhinorrhea), mouth (airway/dental). Unequal pupils in trauma suggests herniation — immediately reassess and expedite transport.
common_errors: Checking pupils without noting size, equality, and reactivity separately
Missing Battle's sign and raccoon eyes as basilar skull fracture indicators

checklist_items.description: Assesses neck for JVD, tracheal midline position, subcutaneous emphysema; applies cervical collar if indicated
checklist_items.done_feedback: Neck assessed and cervical collar applied if indicated — JVD, tracheal position, and subcutaneous emphysema findings documented.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Neck assessment in trauma: JVD suggests tension PTX or pericardial tamponade; tracheal deviation is a late tension PTX sign; subcutaneous emphysema indicates lung or airway injury. Assess neck before applying a collar so you don't obscure findings.
common_errors: Applying collar before assessing neck — covers JVD and tracheal position
Forgetting to check tracheal midline

checklist_items.description: Inspects and palpates chest for deformity, paradoxical motion, crepitus; auscultates bilateral breath sounds
checklist_items.done_feedback: Chest assessed with inspection, palpation, and auscultation — thoracic injuries requiring immediate intervention identified.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Full chest assessment: inspect (symmetry, open wounds, paradoxical motion), palpate (rib tenderness, crepitus, subcutaneous emphysema), auscultate (bilateral breath sounds). Missed pneumothorax or hemothorax can be rapidly fatal — this is a life-threat screen.
common_errors: Auscultating only anteriorly
Missing flail segment under clothing
Not comparing bilateral breath sounds

checklist_items.description: Inspects and palpates abdomen for tenderness, guarding, and rigidity; assesses pelvis for instability (once only)
checklist_items.done_feedback: Abdomen and pelvis assessed — intra-abdominal hemorrhage and pelvic fracture screened.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Abdominal and pelvic assessment: palpate all four quadrants for tenderness and guarding; compress the pelvis once for instability. Pelvic fractures can cause 2–4 L of internal blood loss — early recognition is life-saving. Do not re-compress if instability is found.
common_errors: Compressing pelvis repeatedly after instability detected (worsens hemorrhage)
Missing seat belt sign as indicator of underlying organ injury

checklist_items.description: Assesses bilateral upper and lower extremities for deformity, PMS (pulse, motor function, sensation), and major injury
checklist_items.done_feedback: Extremity PMS assessed bilaterally — vascular compromise and neurological deficits identified.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Assess all four extremities: inspect (deformity, angulation, swelling), palpate (tenderness, crepitus), check PMS (distal pulse, grip strength or plantar flex, light touch) bilaterally. Absent distal pulse = vascular emergency requiring expedited surgical care.
common_errors: Checking motor and sensation without distal pulses
Not comparing bilateral PMS findings

checklist_items.description: Assesses posterior thorax, lumbar spine, and buttocks using log-roll with inline spinal stabilization
checklist_items.done_feedback: Posterior thorax and lumbar assessed — completes the head-to-toe and avoids missed posterior injuries.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Posterior assessment requires log-rolling with maintained spinal precaution. Assess thorax (posterior breath sounds, wounds), spine (step-off, midline spinous process tenderness), and buttocks. Life-threatening posterior wounds and unstable spine fractures are frequently missed without this step.
common_errors: Skipping posterior assessment under time pressure
Not palpating spinous processes for midline tenderness during log-roll

checklist_items.description: Identifies injuries found in secondary assessment and initiates appropriate treatment (splinting, wound management, immobilization)
checklist_items.done_feedback: Injuries identified during secondary assessment treated appropriately.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Injuries found in secondary assessment require treatment: splint long bone fractures (femur requires traction splint), dress open wounds, immobilize impaled objects in place. Treat as you find — do not defer all treatment to the emergency department.
common_errors: Identifying injury verbally but not documenting or treating it

checklist_items.description: Performs and documents reassessment of LOC, vital signs, and injury sites during transport; trends compared to baseline
checklist_items.done_feedback: Reassessment documented during transport — trend data is essential for receiving facility preparation.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: Reassess every 5 minutes for critical patients, every 15 minutes for stable patients, during transport. Document LOC changes, vital sign trends, and response to interventions. Missed deterioration during transport is a preventable failure.
common_errors: Documenting only initial vitals without transport reassessment
Not noting mental status trends en route

checklist_items.description: Provides structured verbal handoff including MOI, injuries found, treatment given, vital sign trends, and patient response
checklist_items.done_feedback: Verbal handoff completed — receiving team briefed on MOI, injuries, interventions, and patient response.
checklist_items.missed_feedback: A structured handoff is required. For trauma: state MOI, injuries found (head-to-toe), vitals on scene and trend, interventions given, patient response, and estimated blood loss if significant. The receiving team cannot prepare the trauma bay without this information.
common_errors: Giving chief complaint only without vital sign trend or treatment summary
Not stating interventions given or patient response to treatment
